turns.content: I'm trying to implement a configuration parser in Rust and I keep hitting the wall with string interning. The docs mention using a StringInterner crate but I don't understand how it actually works under the hood compared to a simple Vec<String>.

Great question. Interning is basically about sharing string instances so that identical strings point to the same memory location, which saves space and speeds up hashing or comparison operations. A StringInterner acts like a global registry where each unique string gets an internal integer ID, while your Vec<String> would just store raw pointers or clones of every distinct value.
So if I have 100k identical config keys, does that mean my heap usage drops significantly? And how do I handle the case where a thread needs to register a new symbol without locking?

Yes, for high cardinality duplicates it can cut memory by half or more since you only allocate once per unique string. For thread safety, most StringInterner implementations use an atomic counter for the next available ID and a separate lock-free list or mutex-protected mapping for actual allocations depending on your performance needs.

That helps with the locking part. But I'm still confused about whether the interned strings are actually owned by my program or just references? Does the original String get dropped if no one holds a reference to it?

Exactly right. The interned string lives in the interner's internal pool until you explicitly drop it, so even if your local variable goes out of scope, the interner keeps it alive as long as any ID is referenced. You never truly own those strings unless you call release or clear on them.

Okay so I should probably use a Symbol type if I want to map these IDs back to actual Strings later, right? And what about the difference between local and global pools in different interner implementations?

Yes, you definitely need a Symbol trait or custom wrapper to handle the ID-to-String mapping cleanly. Local pools are often used for per-process or short-lived scopes where memory is cheap, while global pools persist across program lifetimes and require careful management of drop behavior.
